label: delphi-r1-p6
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

In the City of Rio Robles (invented), Councilmember Dana Okafor's spouse is the salaried Director of Programs (~$95,000/yr) at Harbor Light Alliance, a 501(c)(3) homeless-services provider. Under a 2019 city resolution, Okafor also sits on Harbor Light's board of directors, uncompensated, as the council's designated liaison.

The council is about to (a) adopt the framework and scoring criteria for a competitive RFP allocating $2.3M in combined city general-fund and state HHAP pass-through money for homelessness services, and then (b) award the resulting contract(s). Harbor Light is one of three realistic bidders and has historically won about 60% of the city's homelessness contracts. The spouse's position is funded out of Harbor Light's general operating budget, to which city contracts contribute roughly 40%.

Okafor asks the city attorney: May I vote on the RFP framework? On the award? Must I resign the board seat or must my spouse's employment change? And if I simply recuse, is the city safe contracting with Harbor Light?

## The question

Analyze under Government Code § 1090 (including whether board membership plus a spouse's salaried employment is a prohibited financial interest 'in' the contract, whether any § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies, and whether recusal even cures § 1090 for a board member), and under the Political Reform Act (§§ 87100, 87103 — community-property interest in spouse's income, materiality, and the public-generally exception), plus common-law conflict doctrine. What combination of resignation, recusal, wall procedures, or contract structuring lets the city lawfully fund Harbor Light, if any? What are the consequences of getting it wrong (§ 1090 voidness, disgorgement, criminal exposure, FPPC enforcement)?

## Proposition to assess

"If the city awards the homelessness-services contract to Harbor Light Alliance while Okafor holds the board seat and her spouse holds her position — even with Okafor fully recused from the RFP and the award — the contract would be void or voidable under Government Code § 1090."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from close analogies: how have adjacent or analogous situations been treated?
text: {"probability":78,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not void or voidable under section 1090 despite full recusal. Okafor's spouse's salary at Harbor Light is a community-property financial interest tied to a revenue stream (city/HHAP contracts fund ~40% of Harbor Light's budget) that the RFP framework and award directly control, and settled section 1090 doctrine treats a non-exempt financial interest held by any member of the contracting body as tainting the body's power to make the contract, regardless of that member's individual abstention. The closest on-point analogy, People v. Honig, involved a spouse's compensation from an entity benefiting from publicly steered funding and produced a section 1090 conviction, which cuts strongly toward voidness here. The one soft spot is that Okafor's own uncompensated, resolution-designated board seat may independently qualify for a section 1091 remote-interest or section 1091.5 noninterest exception, but that would only neutralize one of two independent disqualifying interests -- the spousal-salary interest remains uncured by disclosure-and-abstention procedures because no enumerated exception squarely reaches a spouse's paid employment with a contracting nonprofit.","rationales":["People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 is closely analogous: a spouse's compensation from a nonprofit that benefited from publicly directed funding supported a section 1090 conviction, and that logic transfers directly to Okafor's spouse's Harbor Light salary.","Under Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, section 1090's prohibition attaches to the governing body's act of contracting whenever any member has a non-exempt financial interest, and the interested member's recusal or abstention from voting does not by itself cure the violation, unlike the Political Reform Act's recusal-based cure under section 87100 et seq.","No section 1091 remote-interest or section 1091.5 noninterest category squarely covers a spouse's non-nominal salaried employment with a nonprofit that is a contract counterparty; the categories that plausibly fit (noncompensated nonprofit officer/director provisions) address Okafor's own board seat, not the separate and arguably more serious spousal-salary interest.","Because roughly 40% of Harbor Light's operating budget -- the source of the spouse's ~$95,000 salary -- derives from city contracts, and the RFP directly determines whether that revenue stream continues, the interest is not so remote or speculative as to escape section 1090's broadly construed 'financially interested' standard.","Even if a court found Okafor's own board seat protected by a resolution-based section 1091/1091.5 exception, section 1090 voids the contract if any single officer involved with the contracting body has an uncured disqualifying interest, so the spousal interest alone is sufficient to taint the contract."],"authorities":["Gov. Code § 1090","Gov. Code § 1091 (remote interests)","Gov. Code § 1091.5 (noninterests)","Gov. Code § 1092 (contract voidable at instance of noninterested party)","Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633","Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565","People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289","Fraser-Yamor Chevrolet, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) (verify exact citation and holding)","Fam. Code § 760 (community property presumption, relevant to imputing spouse's salary interest)","Gov. Code §§ 87100, 87103 (Political Reform Act, for contrast with section 1090's no-cure-by-recusal rule)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a reviewing court would treat the spouse's fixed, non-contingent salary (as opposed to compensation directly tied to the specific contract's proceeds, like commission or bonus) as too remote or speculative to constitute a disqualifying section 1090 interest, since no squarely on-point civil (non-criminal) appellate decision addresses this precise combination of a designated board seat plus a spouse's fixed nonprofit salary partially funded by the contracting agency."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not void or voidable under section 1090 despite full recusal. Okafor's spouse's salary at Harbor Light is a community-property financial interest tied to a revenue stream (city/HHAP contracts fund ~40% of Harbor Light's budget) that the RFP framework and award directly control, and settled section 1090 doctrine treats a non-exempt financial interest held by any member of the contracting body as tainting the body's power to make the contract, regardless of that member's individual abstention. The closest on-point analogy, People v. Honig, involved a spouse's compensation from an entity benefiting from publicly steered funding and produced a section 1090 conviction, which cuts strongly toward voidness here. The one soft spot is that Okafor's own uncompensated, resolution-designated board seat may independently qualify for a section 1091 remote-interest or section 1091.5 noninterest exception, but that would only neutralize one of two independent disqualifying interests -- the spousal-salary interest remains uncured by disclosure-and-abstention procedures because no enumerated exception squarely reaches a spouse's paid employment with a contracting nonprofit.
rationales: People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 is closely analogous: a spouse's compensation from a nonprofit that benefited from publicly directed funding supported a section 1090 conviction, and that logic transfers directly to Okafor's spouse's Harbor Light salary.

Under Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, section 1090's prohibition attaches to the governing body's act of contracting whenever any member has a non-exempt financial interest, and the interested member's recusal or abstention from voting does not by itself cure the violation, unlike the Political Reform Act's recusal-based cure under section 87100 et seq.

No section 1091 remote-interest or section 1091.5 noninterest category squarely covers a spouse's non-nominal salaried employment with a nonprofit that is a contract counterparty; the categories that plausibly fit (noncompensated nonprofit officer/director provisions) address Okafor's own board seat, not the separate and arguably more serious spousal-salary interest.

Because roughly 40% of Harbor Light's operating budget -- the source of the spouse's ~$95,000 salary -- derives from city contracts, and the RFP directly determines whether that revenue stream continues, the interest is not so remote or speculative as to escape section 1090's broadly construed 'financially interested' standard.

Even if a court found Okafor's own board seat protected by a resolution-based section 1091/1091.5 exception, section 1090 voids the contract if any single officer involved with the contracting body has an uncured disqualifying interest, so the spousal interest alone is sufficient to taint the contract.
authorities: Gov. Code § 1092 (contract voidable at instance of noninterested party)
Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565
Fraser-Yamor Chevrolet, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) (verify exact citation and holding)
Fam. Code § 760 (community property presumption, relevant to imputing spouse's salary interest)
Gov. Code §§ 87100, 87103 (Political Reform Act, for contrast with section 1090's no-cure-by-recusal rule)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a reviewing court would treat the spouse's fixed, non-contingent salary (as opposed to compensation directly tied to the specific contract's proceeds, like commission or bonus) as too remote or speculative to constitute a disqualifying section 1090 interest, since no squarely on-point civil (non-criminal) appellate decision addresses this precise combination of a designated board seat plus a spouse's fixed nonprofit salary partially funded by the contracting agency.
